summary: Gruber examines Apple's removal of VPN apps from the Chinese App Store, pushing back against the simplistic narrative that Apple caved to pressure without legal basis. He argues that defying China's VPN regulations would inevitably lead to Apple exiting the Chinese market entirely, which would leave Chinese users worse off — particularly since iMessage and FaceTime provide end-to-end encryption that still works in China. Rather than framing this as a simple moral failing, he identifies the real structural issue: the App Store's monopoly on iOS software distribution gives totalitarian regimes a single choke point to control what users can install.
key_points: China explicitly announced regulations requiring VPN developers to obtain government licenses, making most VPN services illegal — Apple did cite a legal basis for the removals
Apple defying the VPN removal order would lead to losing the entire Chinese market, not just one policy battle, leaving Chinese iPhone users with useless devices
iMessage and FaceTime remain among the few end-to-end encrypted communication protocols still available in China, a benefit that goes largely unrecognized
The deeper structural problem is the App Store as the sole distribution channel for iOS apps — a design that becomes a tool of authoritarian control
A Gatekeeper-style sideloading option for iOS would eliminate the single choke point, though Apple is unlikely to pursue it
money_quotes: It's easy to say 'Apple shouldn't have removed these apps.' It's not so easy to say 'Apple should pull out of China.'

The App Store was envisioned as a means for Apple to maintain strict control over the software running on iOS devices. But in a totalitarian state like China, it becomes a source of control for the totalitarian regime.
If you're angry about Apple's role in this VPN crackdown in China, I suggest you direct your anger at the App Store as the single source for third-party software.
If you really think VPN apps in the App Store is the hill Apple should die on in China, I get it. But I do not agree.

They'd rather deal with the negative consequences of the App Store as a choke point than give up the benefits (including the profits) of maintaining complete control over all software on the platform.
iMessage and FaceTime are among the few protocols available inside China with end-to-end encryption.
Too many people reacting to this story think that it's about Apple deciding to acquiesce to this particular demand regarding VPN apps. It's not.
key_insight: The real issue isn't whether Apple should comply with China's VPN ban, but whether the App Store's monopoly on iOS software distribution — originally designed for quality control — has become a structural vulnerability that hands authoritarian governments a single lever to control what citizens can use.
